title: Lisa The Iconoclast

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Opening of episode with Homer's signature catchphrase
jokes.punchline: The familiar sound that signals we're in for Homer's antics
jokes.explanation: Homer's signature catchphrase - perfectly integrated to his character and economical, though not original at this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: after misinterpreting a passage in the Bible
jokes.setup: Pioneers leaving Maryland with determination
jokes.punchline: Their motivation is religious misunderstanding, not noble purpose
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons satire of American pioneer mythology - subverts the heroic narrative with religious incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: The pioneers of Maryland were driven by a noble purpose: to establish a haven for religious freedom.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Little did they know, they had misread the map. They thought it said 'Maryland' but it actually said 'Mary's Land.'

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: So they spent the next forty years looking for a woman named Mary.

jokes.text: I hope they show the time where they traded guns to the Indians for corn... and then the Indians shot them and took the corn
jokes.setup: Bart expressing excitement about historical reenactment
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm is for the violence and irony of colonial incompetence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - morbid curiosity about historical violence delivered with childlike enthusiasm. Great character integration and dark humor

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cowabunga! A historical reenactment of colonial times! This is gonna be awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, check it out - muskets misfiring, cannons backfiring into the colonists' own ranks. It's like watching a bunch of idiots accidentally destroy themselves!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The irony is so sweet - they came to conquer and ended up getting owned by their own incompetence. This rules!

jokes.text: It's some sort of land cow
jokes.setup: Settlers encountering what appears to be a monster
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous description of what's clearly a buffalo
jokes.explanation: Economical absurdist description that highlights settler ignorance, though fairly straightforward

dialogue.speaker: Settler 1
dialogue.line: Good heavens! What manner of beast is that?

dialogue.speaker: Settler 2
dialogue.line: It's a massive creature with shaggy brown fur, horns like curved daggers, and a snort that shakes the earth!

dialogue.speaker: Settler 1
dialogue.line: We're doomed! It must be some sort of demon!

dialogue.speaker: Settler 2
dialogue.line: Wait... I think it's just a buffalo.

jokes.text: A noble spirit 'embiggens' the smallest man
jokes.setup: Jebediah giving inspirational advice to a young settler
jokes.punchline: Uses the made-up word 'embiggens' as if it's real and profound
jokes.explanation: Brilliant invented word that sounds profound but is nonsense. Became a cultural reference and perfectly captures the show's wordplay genius

dialogue.speaker: Jebediah Springfield
dialogue.line: A word to the wise: it is no coincidence that 'embiggens' and 'cromulent' have entered the edumbucational lexicon of our people.

jokes.text: 'Embiggens'? Hmm. I never heard that word before I moved to Springfield
jokes.setup: Teacher questioning the validity of 'embiggens'
jokes.punchline: Sets up the next joke about it being 'cromulent'
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the next joke, establishes doubt about the made-up word

dialogue.speaker: Edna Krabappel
dialogue.line: I've never heard of the word 'embiggens' before I moved to Springfield.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: It's a perfectly cromulent word.

dialogue.speaker: Edna Krabappel
dialogue.line: Cromulent?

jokes.text: I don't know why. It's a perfectly 'cromulent' word
jokes.setup: Responding to Edna's doubt about 'embiggens'
jokes.punchline: Defends one made-up word with another made-up word
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the wordplay joke - 'cromulent' is even more absurd but delivered with complete authority

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Well, I've never heard of the word 'embiggens' before I moved to Springfield.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Neither had I. It's a perfectly cromulent word.

jokes.text: with the possible exception of Kearney
jokes.setup: Talking about children being too young to remember the bicentennial
jokes.punchline: Implies Kearney is so old he might remember 1976
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific joke about Kearney being unusually old for elementary school, delivered casually

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Children, you were all born after the bicentennial in 1976.

dialogue.speaker: Kearney
dialogue.line: Were not!

jokes.text: Those tall ships really lifted the nation's spirits after Watergate
jokes.setup: Continuing about the bicentennial celebration
jokes.punchline: Connects superficial patriotic display to serious political scandal
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about how pageantry distracts from real issues

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Children, as part of our bicentennial celebration, we'll be displaying the American flag in front of the school.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool! Nothing says 'America' like a piece of cloth.

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Well, Bart, that flag represents our nation's values and—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom says it's funny how we wrap ourselves in the flag while ignoring real problems.

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Your mother is very wise. Now, where did Principal Skinner put the flag pole? He said something about burying it in the basement with some old financial records.

jokes.text: and the best 18 will be put on file in the school library... available to anyone who requests them
jokes.setup: Describing what happens to the best essays
jokes.punchline: The 'reward' is being filed away where no one will ever see them
jokes.explanation: Good observation about the meaninglessness of school honors

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: The best essays are filed away where no one will ever see them.

jokes.text: Can we do illustrations? - No.
jokes.setup: Student asking hopefully about adding pictures
jokes.punchline: Flat, immediate rejection
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deadpan rejection - captures school bureaucracy's soul-crushing nature

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: Can we add pictures to make it more interesting?

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Look. I can be a butter churner, a typhoid carrier, an apprentice!
jokes.setup: Homer excitedly reading volunteer opportunities
jokes.punchline: Lists 'typhoid carrier' as if it's an appealing job alongside normal roles
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer obliviousness - doesn't realize 'typhoid carrier' isn't a job but a medical condition

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! Look at all these volunteer opportunities! We got... uh... crossing guard, lunch monitor, typhoid carrier...

jokes.text: Well, yeah, Dad. You're a big, fat loudmouth... and you can walk when you have to
jokes.setup: Homer asking if he'd be good as town crier
jokes.punchline: Bart's 'supportive' assessment lists Homer's worst qualities as qualifications
jokes.explanation: Excellent Bart cruelty disguised as encouragement - perfect character moment with great structure

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So you think I'd make a good town crier?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, Dad, you've got all the qualifications. You're loud, obnoxious, you repeat yourself constantly, and nobody really listens to you anyway.

jokes.text: Dangerous river crossings threatened life and limb... but helped our founding fathers save on bridge tolls
jokes.setup: Describing the dangers of river crossings
jokes.punchline: Reduces heroic struggles to penny-pinching
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of historical romanticism with modern economic concerns

dialogue.speaker: Museum Narrator
dialogue.line: Many a valiant pioneer lost their lives attempting to ford the treacherous rivers of the American frontier. Oxen drowned, wagons were swept away, families were torn asunder... all to save the cost of a five-cent toll.

jokes.text: even when I'm drinking my chicory
jokes.setup: Curator explaining his passion for Jebediah
jokes.punchline: Adds the random detail about drinking period-appropriate beverage
jokes.explanation: Nice character detail showing curator's historical immersion lifestyle

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: I've dedicated my life to studying Jebediah Springfield. Every detail matters - from his uniform to his drinking habits. He only consumed beverages that were popular during his era.

jokes.text: but I think you'll find he's easily the equal... of William Dawes or even Samuel Otis
jokes.setup: Curator building up Jebediah's importance
jokes.punchline: Compares him to completely obscure historical figures
jokes.explanation: Clever joke requiring historical knowledge - those are genuinely minor patriots, making the comparison meaningless

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: Jebediah Springfield was many things: a soldier, a statesman, a man of boundless vision. Why, he's as important to American history as... as Benjamin Franklin, as Thomas Jefferson, as... uh... Ulysses S. Kimball!

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer losing the town crier position to Ned
jokes.punchline: His signature disappointed grunt
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer reaction to disappointment - callback to his catchphrase

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Well, thankily-dank, Mayor. I shan't disappoint
jokes.setup: Ned accepting the town crier position
jokes.punchline: His made-up Flanders-speak expressing gratitude
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders linguistic invention - captures his character's earnest awkwardness

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'm tickled pinker than a fresh-caught salmon in a tuxedo! This is more exciting than a double-decker sandwich with extra mustard! I'm so grateful, I could just about plotz-a-rooney! Okily-dokily, I promise to ring this bell with the enthusiasm of a thousand church choirs singing hymns to the Lord above!

jokes.text: 'Har' ye. 'Har' ye. I declare myself pickled tink... about Springfield's bicen-citally... ti-tin-toodily, rin-tin-tennial day!
jokes.setup: Ned attempting to be a town crier
jokes.punchline: Completely mangles the announcement with his nervous Flanders-speak
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Ned's nervousness breaks down his speech into gibberish while maintaining his voice patterns

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Oyez, oyez, oyez! Hear ye, hear ye, um, well, actually, if it's not too much trouble, would folks kindly lend me their ear-a-rooney? The town council has asked me to announce that, uh, the annual springtime festival-diddly-estival will be occurring on Saturday, or perhaps Sunday if that's more convenient for everyone, no pressure-a-rooney!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: And, um, there will be games and refreshments, though of course I must caution that the games are, diddly-deed, entirely voluntary, and if anyone feels uncomfortable participating, well, that's perfectly okey-dokey! We wouldn't want anyone feeling pressured-diddly-essured into having fun, now would we?

jokes.text: You su-diddly-uck, Flanders! Give me that
jokes.setup: Homer angry at Ned's poor town crying
jokes.punchline: Tries to insult Ned but accidentally uses Flanders-speak
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedy collision - Homer's anger gets filtered through Ned's speech pattern, creating absurd hybrid

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Listen here, Flanders! You're nothing but a sanctimonious, Bible-thumping... diddly-doodly... Hey, wait a minute!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Problem-o, Homer-diddly-o?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't you 'diddly-o' me, you... you... okily-dokily, get outta here!

jokes.text: Chooseth Homer Simpson, and he shalt rock thy world
jokes.setup: Homer taking over as town crier
jokes.punchline: Mixes ye olde English with modern slang
jokes.explanation: Good Homer modernizing of historical role - linguistic anachronism that fits his character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oyez, oyez, oyez! Hear ye, hear ye, hear ye! It is I, Homer Simpson, your new town crier, fo' shizzle!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let it be known throughout the land that the town meeting hath been postponed, my bad, homies.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Furthermore, verily and forsooth, the Kwik-E-Mart is having a sick sale on Squishees, yo!

jokes.text: Good God! He is fabulous
jokes.setup: Homer's surprisingly effective town crying
jokes.punchline: Mayor's shocked admiration for Homer's competence
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction joke playing on Homer unexpectedly excelling

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: My heavens! Homer Simpson has single-handedly restored order to our fair town!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I must say, I am absolutely flabbergasted by his competence and dedication to civic duty.

jokes.text: He's 'embiggened' that role with his 'cromulent' performance
jokes.setup: Praising Homer's town crier performance
jokes.punchline: Uses both made-up words from the opening sequence
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the invented words - shows how fake language can become accepted reality

dialogue.speaker: Townsperson
dialogue.line: That was the best town crier performance I've ever seen! You really rang that bell with such passion and authenticity!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks! I learned from the best. Did you hear me say 'D'oh!' and 'Mmm... donuts'? Those are my signature moves!

dialogue.speaker: Townsperson
dialogue.line: Those aren't real words, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They are now!

jokes.text: The Homer broadcasting system is on the air!
jokes.setup: Homer continuing his town crier duties
jokes.punchline: Parodies CBS with his own name
jokes.explanation: Decent pun showing Homer's ego and modernization of the historical role

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hear ye, hear ye! The Simpsons — it's like CBS, but with better ratings!

jokes.text: and his chamber pot
jokes.setup: Curator showing Jebediah's noble artifacts - fife and hatchet
jokes.punchline: Ends the list with bathroom equipment
jokes.explanation: Good anticlimax joke - deflates the reverent tone with crude reality

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: And here we have the fife that Jebediah Springfield played at the Battle of Bloody Clam Cove.

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: And this is the hatchet he used to chop down that cherry tree, which, as we all know, he then lied about.

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: And finally, his chamber pot.

jokes.text: Till 1796 I was Hans Sprungfeld, murderous pirate
jokes.setup: Confession revealing Jebediah had a different identity
jokes.punchline: The respected founder was actually a violent criminal
jokes.explanation: Major plot revelation that's inherently funny - complete inversion of heroic mythology

dialogue.speaker: Jebediah Springfield
dialogue.line: I must confess my terrible secret. I am not the virtuous founder you believe me to be.

dialogue.speaker: Jebediah Springfield
dialogue.line: My real name is Hans Sprungfeld, and I was a dangerous pirate and criminal!

jokes.text: And the half-wits of this town will never learn the truth. Ha, ha, ha, ha, ha! Ha, ha, ha, ha, ha!
jokes.setup: Jebediah's arrogant dismissal of townspeople
jokes.punchline: Evil laughter written out in the confession
jokes.explanation: Good villain characterization - writing out the evil laughter is absurd but effective

dialogue.speaker: Jebediah Springfield
dialogue.line: Bah! These pathetic townspeople are beneath me. They grovel and worship at my feet while I mock them in secret.

dialogue.speaker: Jebediah Springfield
dialogue.line: Mwahahaha! Bwahahaha! Mwahahahahaha!

jokes.text: Sounds like you've come down with a serious case of 'Jebeditis'
jokes.setup: Lisa looking flushed after discovering the confession
jokes.punchline: Curator makes a medical pun about Jebediah obsession
jokes.explanation: Decent pun that fits the curator's personality and sets up the next joke

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: Lisa, you're looking a bit flushed. I'd say your obsession with Jebediah Springfield is giving you a fever... of historical proportions.

jokes.text: Just when I was getting over my Chester A. Arthritis
jokes.setup: Following up the 'Jebeditis' pun
jokes.punchline: Another historical figure/disease pun with obscure president
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of the pun format - Chester A. Arthur is perfectly obscure for this character

jokes.text: I don't understand thee, Marge
jokes.setup: Homer trying to stay in character as town crier
jokes.punchline: Uses formal 'thee' for mundane breakfast conversation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's commitment to his role in inappropriate contexts

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hear ye, hear ye! I bring news of the morning repast!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm the town crier! Oyez, oyez! Let it be known that I shall be consuming thee scrambled eggs and thee toast this fine day!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You mean 'the,' not 'thee.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what I said - thee! Hear ye, hear ye! Thee orange juice approaches thy lips!

jokes.text: No tongue, eh? How did he talk- and eat and laugh and love?
jokes.setup: Learning about Jebediah's missing tongue
jokes.punchline: Homer's romantic escalation ending with 'love'
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation from practical concerns to romantic implications

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Her tongue?! Oh man, I'd give anything to have her tongue in my mouth!

jokes.text: The dastard!
jokes.setup: Learning Jebediah tried to kill Washington
jokes.punchline: Homer's period-appropriate exclamation of outrage
jokes.explanation: Homer staying in historical character while expressing genuine shock

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sweet lion of Damascus!

jokes.text: I got the white stars you wanted, but I couldn't find... any red hearts, yellow moons or green clovers
jokes.setup: Apu reporting on craft supplies for the celebration
jokes.punchline: Lists Lucky Charms cereal marshmallow shapes instead of patriotic decorations
jokes.explanation: Clever product placement parody - connects patriotic decorations to breakfast cereal

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: For the celebration, I have procured the following items: red, white, and blue balloons, patriotic bunting, and... lucky charms marshmallows shaped like stars and horseshoes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marshmallows? Where?

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: These are decoration-grade only, Homer. The stars represent our nation's ideals, and the horseshoes represent... well, also luck.

jokes.text: they had a choice of living in Springfield or Stenchburg
jokes.setup: Marge explaining why her family chose Springfield
jokes.punchline: The alternative was a town literally named for bad smell
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist town name that makes Springfield seem appealing by comparison

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The alternative was a town literally named for bad smell.

jokes.text: And, Lisa, for your- essay... 'Jebediah Springfield: Super-Fraud,' 'F'
jokes.setup: Teacher grading Lisa's essay
jokes.punchline: The inflammatory title gets an F despite being true
jokes.explanation: Good satire of how truth can be punished when it challenges accepted narratives

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: Lisa, your essay on the history of American imperialism is well-researched, but the title 'America: A Nation of Warmongering Hypocrites' is far too inflammatory.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Ms. Hoover, everything in that essay is factually accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: I'm sure it is, dear. That's why you get an F.

jokes.text: It's women like you who keep the rest of us from landing a husband
jokes.setup: Ms. Hoover continuing her rant at Lisa
jokes.punchline: Blames her own romantic failures on Lisa's academic honesty
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ms. Hoover's personal bitterness affecting her teaching

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: This is your fault, Lisa. Your constant academic honesty and integrity have made me look bad in front of the other teachers. If you hadn't been so honest, I could have covered up my failures and maybe Principal Skinner would have noticed me romantically instead of always going after the pretty young substitute teachers. But no, your perfect student ethics had to ruin everything for me.

jokes.text: Well, I've been called a greasy thug too, and it never stops hurting
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Lisa about being called a 'P.C. thug'
jokes.punchline: Relates it to his own experience as a literal greasy thug
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer misdirection - thinks he's being supportive but reveals he actually is what he was called

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, don't worry about it, Lis. I've been called worse things than that. Why, just last week someone called me a greasy thug.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's different. They were being literal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, but it still hurt.

jokes.text: Grease ourselves up real good and trash that place with a baseball bat
jokes.setup: Homer suggesting how to deal with the school situation
jokes.punchline: His solution is literal violent thuggery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer solution - completely inappropriate escalation to violence with the greasing detail

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here's what we do. We get a gun, we go to that school, and we just start blasting.

jokes.text: One: Where's the fife? And two: Give me the fife
jokes.setup: Homer questioning the curator
jokes.punchline: His two questions are essentially the same demand
jokes.explanation: Great Homer logic - pretends to have multiple questions but really just wants one thing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So you're telling me this painting is worth $5 million?

dialogue.speaker: Curator
dialogue.line: Yes, it's a priceless masterpiece.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And if I were to steal it, how much could I get for it on the black market?

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? Well, put this in your fife and smoke it
jokes.setup: Homer responding to jurisdiction challenge
jokes.punchline: Adapts 'put that in your pipe and smoke it' to the fife context
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun adaptation that fits the moment perfectly - clever wordplay that feels natural

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Put that in your fife and smoke it!

jokes.text: The so-called confession... is just as phony as the Howard Hughes will... the Hitler diaries or the Emancipation Retraction
jokes.setup: Curator listing famous historical hoaxes
jokes.punchline: Ends with completely made-up 'Emancipation Retraction'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation mixing real hoaxes with absurd invented one - 'Emancipation Retraction' is perfectly ridiculous

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: And finally, the Emancipation Retraction of 1867, where President Johnson declared all slaves to be slaves again.

jokes.text: You and your children and your children's children... For three months
jokes.setup: Curator dramatically banning Lisa's family line
jokes.punchline: The eternal ban is actually just three months
jokes.explanation: Perfect anticlimax - builds up biblical-level punishment then deflates to bureaucratic pettiness

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: Your family is hereby banned from the Springfield Museum of Natural History... for all eternity!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All eternity? That's like... what, three months?

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlbut
dialogue.line: Yes, actually. Three months.

jokes.text: Okay. 100 yellow
jokes.setup: Lisa ordering copies in multiple specific yellow shades
jokes.punchline: Worker reduces it to generic 'yellow'
jokes.explanation: Good workplace humor about ignoring customer specifications

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: I need copies in Goldenrod, Mustard, Canary, and Chartreuse.

dialogue.speaker: Print Shop Worker
dialogue.line: So... yellow?

jokes.text: And you're the littlest guy I know
jokes.setup: Homer explaining he always helps the little guy
jokes.punchline: Lisa is literally the smallest person he knows
jokes.explanation: Sweet Homer logic - takes metaphorical concept literally in endearing way

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I always help the little guy.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm literally the smallest person you know.

jokes.text: Question. Is your name Ridley Scott or James Cameron? - No. It's Homer
jokes.setup: Man suspiciously questioning Homer about his identity
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest, literal response to paranoid accusation
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between paranoid writer and oblivious Homer

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Who are you? Are you a cop?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, I'm not a cop.

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Are you sure?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm sure. I'm Homer Simpson.

jokes.text: And if I see a movie where computers threaten our personal liberties... I will know that you stole my idea
jokes.setup: Writer protecting his screenplay idea
jokes.punchline: The 'original' concept is incredibly generic sci-fi
jokes.explanation: Good satire of Hollywood paranoia over unoriginal ideas

dialogue.speaker: Screenplay writer
dialogue.line: You can't tell anyone about my screenplay idea. It's completely original.

dialogue.speaker: Screenplay writer
dialogue.line: It's about a group of rebels who have to stop an evil empire from destroying the galaxy.

jokes.text: Mental note. Steal his idea
jokes.setup: Homer hearing the writer's paranoid warning
jokes.punchline: Immediately decides to do exactly what the writer fears
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - completely validates the writer's paranoia through oblivious honesty

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, so if I do this, something bad will happen?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well then I'm definitely doing it!

jokes.text: Oh. You're just as sweet as the sticks which bear your name
jokes.setup: Apu responding warmly to Lisa
jokes.punchline: References Lisa as 'sweet as candy canes' in his flowery way
jokes.explanation: Nice Apu wordplay connecting Lisa's name to candy - fits his poetic speaking style

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Oh, Lisa, you are as sweet as candy canes hanging from the boughs of a Christmas tree in a perfect world where nothing bad ever happens and everyone loves each other.

jokes.text: as red hot as the candies which bear that name
jokes.setup: Apu explaining why he can't help with the poster
jokes.punchline: Another candy reference to describe his immigration predicament
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Apu's candy metaphor theme, though less effective than the first

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I cannot help with the poster. You see, I am an illegal immigrant, and if I am caught, I will be deported back to India. It is a bit like being a Whipple—once you're caught, you're finished!

jokes.text: Aw, the little cutie wants to do somethin' cute
jokes.setup: Homer announcing Lisa has something important to say
jokes.punchline: Barney's condescending dismissal of Lisa's serious message
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the contrast with Lisa's shocking revelation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa has something important to say.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have something important to say.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's nice, sweetie. Now run along and play with your dolls.

jokes.text: Homer, you know, I support, uh, most any prejudice you can name... but your hero phobia sickens me
jokes.setup: Moe expressing disapproval of Homer and Lisa
jokes.punchline: He's fine with all prejudices except being against heroes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Moe character moment - casually admits to supporting all prejudices while drawing the line at 'hero phobia'

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: He's fine with all prejudices except being against heroes

jokes.text: There's an exit?
jokes.setup: Moe telling Barney to show them the exit
jokes.punchline: Barney is surprised the bar has an exit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Barney moment - implies he's been trapped in Moe's forever

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Show them the exit, Barney.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: The bar has an exit?

jokes.text: A pirate? Well, that's hardly the image we want for Long John Silver's
jokes.setup: Learning Jebediah was a pirate
jokes.punchline: Long John Silver's restaurant doesn't want pirate association
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony - restaurant named after famous pirate doesn't want pirate connections

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Sponsor
dialogue.line: We're pulling our sponsorship. We can't be associated with a pirate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But what about Long John Silver's?

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Sponsor
dialogue.line: Especially them.

jokes.text: Can't we have one meeting that doesn't end with us digging up a corpse?
jokes.setup: Town committee deciding to dig up Jebediah's grave
jokes.punchline: Implies this happens regularly at town meetings
jokes.explanation: Fantastic absurdist line suggesting Springfield regularly digs up graves at meetings - perfectly weird

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: All in favor of exhuming the body of our founder, Jebediah Springfield?

dialogue.speaker: Townspeople
dialogue.line: [multiple hands raise]

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Motion carries. This concludes our meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Same time next week?

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Of course.

jokes.text: Credibility. - Aw. What a gyp
jokes.setup: Homer asking what they win if Lisa is right
jokes.punchline: Homer disappointed that truth only offers credibility
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment valuing prizes over truth - typical materialistic response

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What do we win if you're right?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The truth.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The truth? What good is that? I wanted a toaster!

jokes.text: Jebediah Springfield has been replaced with a skeleton!
jokes.setup: Opening Jebediah's coffin
jokes.punchline: Wiggum thinks skeletons aren't normal in graves
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum stupidity - doesn't understand basic decomposition

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Good Lord! A skeleton! That's not normal, is it?

jokes.text: Don't forget to look in his shoes. When I lose something, sometimes it turns up in my shoes
jokes.setup: Searching skeleton for silver tongue
jokes.punchline: Homer applies his personal experience to 200-year-old corpse
jokes.explanation: Great Homer logic applying modern personal habits to historical investigation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at this skeleton. No silver tongue. I know what that's like.

jokes.text: Well, that's the spirit, Bonesy
jokes.setup: Talking to the skeleton after Lou's ventriloquism
jokes.punchline: Wiggum treats the skeleton like it's actually talking
jokes.explanation: Good Wiggum gullibility - completely fooled by obvious ventriloquism

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now listen here, you bony wise guy. I don't appreciate your smart mouth.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You better watch it, or I'll run you in for vagrancy.

jokes.text: General Washington?
jokes.setup: Lisa having a dream
jokes.punchline: George Washington appears as a ghost to encourage her
jokes.explanation: Standard dream sequence setup with historical figure guidance

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: George Washington?

dialogue.speaker: George Washington
dialogue.line: That's right, Lisa. I've come to encourage you. You must never give up on your dreams.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But how did you...?

dialogue.speaker: George Washington
dialogue.line: I'm a ghost, Lisa. I can do anything.

jokes.text: We had quitters in the Revolution too. We called them Kentuckians
jokes.setup: Washington responding to Lisa's desire to quit
jokes.punchline: Casual historical insult of Kentucky
jokes.explanation: Good historical burn - Washington casually insulting an entire state

dialogue.speaker: George Washington
dialogue.line: Quit? Lisa, our forefathers didn't quit. We fought the British, we crossed the Delaware in the dead of winter, and we didn't complain about Kentucky.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What's wrong with Kentucky?

dialogue.speaker: George Washington
dialogue.line: Have you ever been there?

jokes.text: What's your friend Janey's number? - No! Not Janey! She'll pack the Supreme Court with boys!
jokes.setup: Washington threatening to find another girl to be president
jokes.punchline: Lisa's specific fear about Janey's judicial appointments
jokes.explanation: Excellent Lisa character moment showing her political awareness and competitive nature

dialogue.speaker: George Washington
dialogue.line: I'll find another girl to be president!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No! Not Janey! She'll appoint her friends to the Supreme Court!

jokes.text: I want to help you, George Washington? Even your dreams are square
jokes.setup: Lisa talking in her sleep about helping Washington
jokes.punchline: Bart's mockery of her even-while-sleeping nerdiness
jokes.explanation: Perfect sibling dynamic - Bart can't resist mocking Lisa even during her dreams

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm coming, Mr. Washington, I'm coming...

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Even in her sleep, she's a little nerd.

jokes.text: Can you open my milk, Mommy? - I'm not Mommy, Ralph. I'm Miss Hoover
jokes.setup: Ralph asking for help with milk
jokes.punchline: He thinks his teacher is his mother
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph confusion showing his disconnect from reality

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Ms. Hoover, I'm leaking!

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: It's just a nosebleed, Ralph.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: But I want my mommy!

dialogue.speaker: Ms. Hoover
dialogue.line: I am your mother.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! My mommy's the best!

jokes.text: When I pried open the coffin it was there- that shiny tongue sticking out of his mouth... razzing my entire career- my-my life
jokes.setup: Curator confessing to stealing the tongue
jokes.punchline: Describes the tongue as 'razzing' him like it was intentionally mocking
jokes.explanation: Great visual and character moment - anthropomorphizes the tongue as deliberately taunting

dialogue.speaker: Hollis Hurlburt
dialogue.line: I couldn't help myself. That tongue, it was just sitting there, razzing me. Mocking me with its very existence.

jokes.text: Jebediah was... great
jokes.setup: Lisa about to reveal the truth about Jebediah
jokes.punchline: She changes her mind and lies to protect the town
jokes.explanation: Powerful character moment - Lisa choosing community harmony over truth

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I was going to tell everyone the truth about Jebediah Springfield, but I can't. This town needs its heroes.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So I'm going to lie and say he was exactly what everyone thinks he was.

jokes.text: Because the myth of Jebediah has value too. It's brought out the best in everyone in this town
jokes.setup: Homer asking why Lisa didn't tell the truth
jokes.punchline: Lisa's wise observation about the value of inspiring myths
jokes.explanation: Profound Lisa moment showing mature understanding of truth vs. beneficial mythology

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why didn't you tell the truth?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Sometimes a inspiring myth is more valuable than the truth.

jokes.text: Regardless of who said it, a noble spirit embiggens the smallest man.
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining the value of myths
jokes.punchline: Callback to the fake inspirational quote as genuinely meaningful
jokes.explanation: Excellent thematic callback that gives genuine meaning to the earlier absurd word

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Myths are an important part of our culture. They teach us valuable lessons about life and human nature.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's why it's so meaningful when we realize the quote we've been living by actually came from a fortune cookie.

jokes.text: Well, I'd like to, ma'am, but he's too damn good
jokes.setup: Woman complaining about Homer's unauthorized town crying
jokes.punchline: Police refuse to stop him because of his excellent performance
jokes.explanation: Good police incompetence joke - quality trumps legality

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Officer, you have to stop Homer from being the town crier! He doesn't have a license!

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Ma'am, we can't stop him.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Have you heard him? The man's a natural. We'd be fools to shut down that kind of talent.

tmdb_overview: While researching into Jebediah Springfield's past for an essay, Lisa discovers that the town founder wasn't the hero that everyone thinks he was.

guest_stars.name: Tress MacNeille
guest_stars.character: Woman in Film / Betsy Ross / Mrs. Glick (voice)

insight: Lisa's historical debunking carries the episode—68 jokes in 22 minutes sustain sharp satirical momentum throughout.
